title: Wintering pigs with extra bedding
subtitle: How to keep outdoor pigs warm and dry through a cold, wet winter.
excerpt: Pigs tolerate cold better than heat, but they cannot maintain body temperature on wet ground with no shelter. A deep, dry bed inside a well-positioned arc is the simplest and most effective way to keep outdoor pigs comfortable from November to March.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, outdoor pig welfare in winter. NADIS, pig housing and environment factsheet. Soil Association, organic pig production standards, housing section.

content.text: bed is more effective than any heating solution for outdoor pigs. Pigs generate considerable body heat; the arc traps it, and the deep straw insulates from the cold ground.

Face the arc entrance away from the prevailing wind direction. In the UK this means the entrance typically faces south-east or south, with the solid back to the south-west or west. An arc facing into the wind will fill with rain and lose all the warm, dry benefit of the bedding. On sloping ground, position the arc so water drains away from rather than into the entrance.

Start the season with a fresh bed of 20 to 30 cm of dry straw. Add a layer of fresh straw every two to three days, or whenever the top of the bed is damp. Do not remove the lower layers during winter; the composting material generates warmth. The pigs will root and settle the layers, and the bed will compress over weeks. By mid-winter, a well-maintained arc bed will be 40 to 50 cm deep and warm to the touch at the lower layers.

Pigs that are cold huddle tightly together, often shivering. Pigs that are comfortable lie spread out in contact with each other but without piling. Check the bedding temperature with the back of your hand pressed into the lower layers; it should feel distinctly warm. If the bed feels cold and wet throughout, remove it all and start fresh with dry straw.

Check water twice daily in freezing weather. A pig that cannot access water for 12 hours will reduce feed intake, which further reduces its ability to maintain body temperature. Break ice from drinkers at each check. If a pipe freezes, carry water manually rather than leaving pigs without until a thaw.
